name: Bhartrprapanca
dates_notes: Known only via Śaṅkara's polemic in the Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Bhāṣya; no surviving work; reconstruction by Hiriyanna 1924 from citation-fragments.
dating_methodology: Bhartṛprapañca is dated by citation-evidence and by inference from the polemical position he holds in Śaṅkara's *Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Bhāṣya*. Terminus ante quem: Śaṅkara (early eighth century, conventionally circa 700–750 CE) treats Bhartṛprapañca as the principal pre-Śaṅkara *bhedābheda* opponent across many chapters of the *Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Bhāṣya*; he must therefore antedate Śaṅkara by enough generations that his position circulated as the established *pūrvapakṣa*. Sureśvara's *Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Vārttika* expands Śaṅkara's refutations and adds further attributed positions, confirming that Bhartṛprapañca was a recognized name in the post-Śaṅkara polemical memory. Terminus post quem: the position presupposes a developed *bhedābheda*-leaning Vedānta inheriting the Mīmāṃsā analysis of *jñāna-karma-samuccaya*, which is mature only by the late fifth century. The window 400–600 CE follows Hiriyanna's reconstruction in 'Bhartṛprapañca: An Old Vedāntin' (*The Indian Antiquary* 53, 1924) and is broadly accepted by Nakamura (*A History of Early Vedānta Philosophy*, vol. II, 1949/1983), Mayeda (*A Thousand Teachings*, 1979, introduction), and the Hacker school. The estimate is bracketed by two anchor-points: the established Vedānta-Mīmāṃsā controversy on the lower side, the necessity of pre-Śaṅkara doctrinal currency on the upper side. Note (AI-generated audit): every dating claim about Bhartṛprapañca is inferred from the polemical record. Hiriyanna's reconstruction methodology in the 1924 *Indian Antiquary* article is the foundational scholarly act here — he reads the Śaṅkara, Sureśvara, and Ānandagiri citations together, identifies stable doctrinal commitments across them (the dual-aspect ontology, *jñāna-karma-samuccaya*, the eight-aspect *prapañca*, the real *aṃśa*-relation of *jīva* to Brahman), and treats consistency of attribution across multiple sources as evidence that an actual textual source is being cited rather than a generic *pūrvapakṣa* being constructed. The methodology is defensible but unverifiable; no manuscript has surfaced to confirm the reconstruction.

lineage_polemical.note: Śaṅkara's Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Bhāṣya is the principal source for and refutation of Bhartṛprapañca's bhedābheda.

core_thesis:
Bhartṛprapañca holds that the *Bṛhadāraṇyaka* teaches an irreducibly two-aspect ontology: *brahman* is intrinsically both the unitary, formless substrate and the genuinely differentiated manifold of name-and-form. The position is *bhedābheda* in a precise structural sense. Both the apophatic *neti neti* of Yājñavalkya (BṛU 2.3.6) and the cosmogonic affirmations of the same teacher (BṛU 1.4.7, 'this was unmanifest; it was differentiated by name and form') are taken as literal scriptural assertions, neither sublatable to the other. The technical move that holds the position together is *aupacārika abhinnatva* — figurative non-difference. The non-difference of self and *brahman* asserted in *aham brahmāsmi* (BṛU 1.4.10) is not numerical identity (the Advaita reading) and not denial of distinctness (the dualist reading); it is the genuine non-difference of a real mode from its substance, which can be spoken of as identity in the figurative-and-true sense in which a wave is non-different from the ocean. The figurative-but-true status is the heart of the doctrine: the abheda-statements are literally true in the *aupacārika* mode, while the bheda-statements are literally true in the *mukhya* (primary, distinguished-mode) mode, and both modes are constitutive of *brahman*'s being.

Four commitments appear stably across the citation-fragments. First, *brahman* genuinely transforms (*pariṇāma*) into *nāma-rūpa* without ceasing to be the unitary substrate; the transformation is real, not apparent (*vivarta*). Second, the *jīva* is a real *aṃśa* of *brahman*, so the upaniṣadic identity-statements are non-canceling but also non-collapsing: the part is genuinely the substance under one description, genuinely a differentiated portion under another. Third, *brahman* is articulable in eight aspects (*aṣṭa-rūpa*) — a structured taxonomy preserved obliquely through the polemical record, in which the differentiation of *brahman* into intelligible modes is itself a feature of *brahman*'s reality and not a feature of the cognizer's limitation. Fourth, liberation requires *jñāna-karma-samuccaya*: the scriptural cognition that recovers the abheda is not an instantaneous event terminating ignorance, but the culmination of *śravaṇa-manana-nididhyāsana-upāsanā* (BṛU 2.4.5; 1.4.15), the four-fold cultivation in which hearing, reflection, deep meditation, and continuing contemplative worship together constitute the path. *Karma* is not subordinated to or dropped at *jñāna*; the two aspects of the path mirror the two aspects of *brahman*.

The philosophical force of the position is that it refuses two later moves at once. Against Śaṅkara, Bhartṛprapañca refuses to treat the cosmogonic affirmations as concessive teachings (*adhyāropa*) to be canceled by the apophatic ones; the manifold is real because *brahman* is real-as-manifold. Against developed dualism, he refuses to treat the apophatic affirmations as predications about a Brahman categorially distinct from the differentiated world; the abheda is real because *brahman* is real-as-substance. The dual-aspect structure is the load-bearing architecture: the position takes seriously both registers of upaniṣadic teaching and treats their joint truth as the condition of the doctrine's adequacy. *Mukti* is the recognition of one's identity as a genuine differentiated mode of *brahman* — not the cancellation of the manifold, but the recovery of the abheda-aspect that ordinary cognition occludes. The *jñāna-karma-samuccaya* path is not a compromise between knowledge-only and action-only; it is the practical correlate of the dual-aspect ontology, in which the cultivation appropriate to recognizing oneself as *aṃśa* must include both the cognitive recovery of the abheda and the continued performance of the actions appropriate to the bheda.

Hiriyanna's 1924 reconstruction methodology, in 'Bhartṛprapañca: An Old Vedāntin' (*The Indian Antiquary* 53), is itself part of what can be philosophically engaged. Hiriyanna identifies stable doctrinal commitments by reading the Śaṅkara, Sureśvara, and Ānandagiri citations together and treating consistency of attribution as evidence of an actual textual source. The methodology is defensible: the four commitments listed above appear repeatedly across independent witnesses, and the position they articulate is dialectically too coherent to be a polemical fabrication. The methodology is also limited: every fragment passes through a hostile filter, the *aṣṭa-rūpa* taxonomy is preserved only in fragmentary form, and the precise specification of *aupacārika abhinnatva* is supplied as much by Hiriyanna's reconstruction as by the citation-record. The honest position is that Bhartṛprapañca is recoverable as a structured *bhedābheda* with the four commitments listed, and that the further metaphysical specification is reconstruction rather than recovery.

engaged_works.title: Reconstructed Brhadaranyaka commentary fragments
engaged_works.ascription_notes: No work survives; the corpus is a modern reconstruction (Hiriyanna 1924) from citations preserved in Śaṅkara, Sureśvara, and Ānandagiri.
engaged_works.summary: Not a work but a reconstruction. No commentary by Bhartṛprapañca survives in any manuscript. The corpus, such as it is, was assembled by M. Hiriyanna in his 1924 article 'Bhartṛprapañca: An Old Vedāntin' (*The Indian Antiquary* 53), and supplemented by later studies (Nakamura, *A History of Early Vedānta Philosophy*, vol. II, 1949/1983; Mayeda, *A Thousand Teachings*, 1979, introduction). The fragments come from four kinds of source. First, Śaṅkara's *Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Upaniṣad-Bhāṣya*, where Bhartṛprapañca is the principal *pūrvapakṣin* across many chapters and is named explicitly. Second, Sureśvara's *Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Vārttika*, which expands Śaṅkara's refutations and preserves further passages. Third, Ānandagiri's sub-commentary, which occasionally labels unattributed positions as Bhartṛprapañca's. Fourth, scattered references in later Vedāntic literature. The reconstruction yields a *Bṛhadāraṇyaka* commentary that took the Upaniṣad to teach a structured *bhedābheda* organized around four interlocking commitments: a real-transformation (*pariṇāma*) of *brahman* into *nāma-rūpa* without cancellation of the unitary substrate; the *jīva* as real *aṃśa* of *brahman*; an eight-aspect (*aṣṭa-rūpa*) articulation of *brahman*; and *jñāna-karma-samuccaya* as the path. The technical move that holds the position together is *aupacārika abhinnatva* — figurative non-difference — which makes the upaniṣadic identity-statements literally true in the figurative-and-true mode of a real mode's non-difference from its substance, while leaving the bheda-statements equally literally true under the primary-mode description. The reading is plausible on internal grounds: the *Bṛhadāraṇyaka* combines cosmogonic affirmation in Yājñavalkya's enumerations (1.4.7) with the apophatic *neti neti* (2.3.6) and the identity-statement *aham brahmāsmi* (1.4.10), and a *bhedābheda* reading of this combination is not a polemical caricature but a coherent interpretive option. The text-historical situation, however, remains irreducibly speculative. Every fragment passes through a hostile filter, and what is called 'Bhartṛprapañca's doctrine' is in the first instance a Śaṅkaran or Sureśvaran summary, not an authorial voice. Hiriyanna's reconstruction methodology — treating consistency of attribution across multiple independent witnesses as evidence that an actual textual source is being cited — is the foundational scholarly act, and remains the basis on which all subsequent engagement proceeds.
engaged_works.source_status_note: Sanskrit text not on disk in clean form; every fragment is cited via Hiriyanna 1924 Indian Antiquary vol 53 reconstruction (image-only PDF on disk) and further-attested via Nakamura 1983, A History of Early Vedanta Philosophy I.7.

key_passages.locus_long: Reconstructed Bṛhadāraṇyaka commentary fragments, Hiriyanna 1924, on Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 1.4.7
key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
tad dha idaṃ tarhy avyākṛtam āsīt ।
tan nāmarūpābhyām eva vyākriyata ॥
key_passages.text_critical_note: Reconstruction-only entry: the thinker's own prose is lost; Hiriyanna's article anchors the doctrine at this Bṛhadāraṇyaka locus, and the Sanskrit is normalized from local Vedānta witnesses rather than taken from authorial autograph.

samasa_vigrahas.note: privative compound for the unmanifest state
the pair through which manifestation becomes determinate

verb_modality.note: middle/passive value in context: manifestation becomes articulated

key_passages.english_close: Then this was unmanifest. It was differentiated precisely by name and form.
key_passages.why_this_passage: Hiriyanna's reconstruction makes Bhartṛprapañca's realism about manifestation turn on exactly this transition from the unmanifest to articulated nāma-rūpa. It is a fair anchor because the surviving evidence repeatedly presents him as taking such cosmological passages literally, not as merely provisional concessions.
key_passages.source_edition: M. Hiriyanna, 'Bhartṛprapañca: An Old Vedāntin,' The Indian Antiquary 53 (1924), reconstruction article; locus normalized against local Vedānta citation witnesses.

key_passages.locus_long: Reconstructed Bṛhadāraṇyaka commentary fragments, Hiriyanna 1924, on Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 2.3.1 and 2.3.6
key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
dve vāva brahmaṇo rūpe mūrtaṃ caivāmūrtaṃ ca ।
neti neti ॥
key_passages.text_critical_note: This is a reconstructed lemma-cluster rather than continuous surviving commentary. Hiriyanna uses these loci to show that Bhartṛprapañca reads the positive two-form teaching and the apophatic 'neti neti' together, not competitively.
key_passages.english_close: Brahman has, indeed, two forms: the formed and the formless. 'Not this, not this.'

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: 'not this, not this,' the apophatic denial of exhaustive predication

key_passages.why_this_passage: This is the clearest on-disk anchor for Hiriyanna's claim that Bhartṛprapañca does not flatten the Upaniṣad into either sheer plurality or sheer negation. It fairly represents him because his distinctiveness lies in insisting that the positive dual-aspect teaching and the apophatic limit-statement are both philosophically operative.
key_passages.source_edition: M. Hiriyanna, 'Bhartṛprapañca: An Old Vedāntin,' The Indian Antiquary 53 (1924), reconstruction article; loci normalized against local Vedānta citation witnesses.

key_passages.locus_long: Reconstructed Bṛhadāraṇyaka commentary fragments, Hiriyanna 1924, on Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 1.4.10
key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
ahaṃ brahmāsmi ।
atha yo 'nyāṃ devatām upāste 'nyo 'sāv anyo 'ham asmīti na sa veda ॥
key_passages.text_critical_note: Reconstruction-only entry: Hiriyanna treats Bhartṛprapañca as fully accepting the abheda-vākyas, but not in a way that cancels the real manifold elsewhere. The passage is therefore selected as a doctrinal anchor, not as proof of later Advaitic reduction.
key_passages.english_close: I am Brahman. But he who worships another deity, thinking 'He is one thing and I am another,' does not know.
key_passages.why_this_passage: Hiriyanna's reconstruction is explicit that Bhartṛprapañca accepts the identity-statements and is not simply a pluralist counter-voice to them. This passage is fair because it shows the anti-separative force he must accommodate, while his distinct doctrine consists in refusing to treat that force as requiring the unreality of manifestation.
key_passages.source_edition: M. Hiriyanna, 'Bhartṛprapañca: An Old Vedāntin,' The Indian Antiquary 53 (1924), reconstruction article; locus normalized against local Vedānta citation witnesses.

key_passages.locus_long: Reconstructed Bṛhadāraṇyaka commentary fragments, Hiriyanna 1924, on Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 2.4.5 and 1.4.15
key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
ātmā vā are draṣṭavyaḥ śrotavyo mantavyo nididhyāsitavyaḥ ।
ātmānam eva lokam upāsīta ॥
key_passages.text_critical_note: This is a reconstruction-cluster: no continuous Bhartṛprapañca autograph survives, and Hiriyanna's article uses precisely such Bṛhadāraṇyaka loci to explain why his soteriology cannot be reduced to instant sentence-cognition alone.

karaka_structure.note: predicate accusative: the self as the world

key_passages.english_close: The self, dear one, is to be seen, to be heard about, to be reflected upon, and to be meditated upon. One should worship the self alone as the world.
key_passages.why_this_passage: This is the fairest on-disk anchor for the reconstructed claim that Bhartṛprapañca does not make liberation hinge on a single bare identity-sentence heard once. Hiriyanna's reconstruction repeatedly turns on the continuing force of hearing, reflection, meditation, and upāsanā; the stronger jñāna-karma-samuccaya thesis is preserved only indirectly in the later polemical record.
key_passages.source_edition: M. Hiriyanna, 'Bhartṛprapañca: An Old Vedāntin,' The Indian Antiquary 53 (1924), reconstruction article; loci normalized against local Vedānta citation witnesses.

quoted_by.what_quoted: Bhartṛprapañca as the principal pre-Śaṅkara *bhedābheda* *pūrvapakṣin* engaged across many chapters; his dual-aspect *pariṇāma*-thesis, the *aṣṭa-rūpa* taxonomy, *aupacārika abhinnatva*, and *jñāna-karma-samuccaya* are reconstructed by Hiriyanna 1924 from these refutation-passages.
quoted_by.on_disk_note: Direct text-grep of 'bhartṛprapañca' returns 0 hits in the GRETIL ed. of the BU-with-Śaṅkara on disk; the orthography in the GRETIL file may differ. Hiriyanna's 1924 reconstruction is on disk in image-only PDF form (/materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/bhartrprapanca_hiriyanna_indian_antiquary_vol53_1924_archive.pdf) and remains the canonical secondary source.

quoted_by.what_quoted: Sureśvara's *Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Vārttika* expands Śaṅkara's refutations of Bhartṛprapañca and adds further attributed positions.
